content:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

5 min read
Just now

--

I’ll be honest — a few months ago I genuinely thought AI had solved web development for me.
Open the editor, describe what I want, watch the code appear. Landing pages, animations, responsive layouts — all of it. For someone just starting out, that feeling hits different. Like someone handed you the keys to a car before you’d even learned to walk.
And I’m not going to pretend AI isn’t impressive. It absolutely is.
But here’s the thing nobody told me: building fast and understanding what you built are two completely different things. I had to find that out the hard way.

The trap looks like progress
When you’re new to web development, everything is slow. HTML, then CSS, then JavaScript, then React, then routing, deployment, APIs — the list keeps growing and nobody gives you a map.
AI changes that overnight.
You type: “Build me a modern portfolio website” — and within seconds, there’s actual code. The page opens. The design looks clean. The buttons work. You feel like a developer.
Then you try to change one small thing.
Maybe you want to move a section. Maybe the mobile layout breaks on your phone. Maybe npm throws an error that looks like it was written by a furious robot who hates beginners.
That’s when it hits you.
You don’t actually understand the code in front of you. And that feeling is genuinely uncomfortable.

Speed without ownership
This is the thing I wish someone had told me before I started.
When AI writes your code, the code exists — but you don’t own it yet. Ownership isn’t the files on your computer. Ownership is knowing:

• Why this file exists

• What this component actually does

• How data moves through the app

• Why something broke

• What else will break if you touch it
Without that, your project is fragile. It looks finished from the outside. But you’re terrified to change anything. That’s not confidence — it’s just a very pretty glass house.

The real cost nobody talks about
I expected AI to make mistakes. That part didn’t surprise me.
What surprised me was how small mistakes snowball when you don’t understand the structure.
AI might put a component in the wrong folder. It might use a library you never installed. It might fix one bug by quietly creating three others. Sometimes it gives you code that works today and makes zero sense in two weeks.
And because everything was generated so fast, you don’t remember why any of it was written that way. You just have this big codebase staring back at you.
I call it the “fast now, confused forever” problem.

Stop using AI as a magic button
I still think AI is one of the best things that’s happened to web development learning. I genuinely do.
But beginners — and I include past-me in this — use it completely wrong.
Wrong way:
“Build me the full website.”
Better way:
“Help me build just the hero section first. Explain every file you create. Keep it simple. Don’t add libraries unless absolutely necessary.”
That one change matters more than any other advice I can give.
When AI builds everything at once, you’re a passenger. When it builds one piece at a time and explains the decisions, you’re driving.
Driving is slower at first. But at least you know where the brakes are.

What actually works for me now
I describe the goal specifically before writing a single line of code.
Not “make a website” — but something like: “Create a landing page for a local AC repair service. Hero section, services list, testimonials, contact button, mobile responsive.”
Then I ask AI to give me a plan first, before any code. This sounds like an extra step. It saves enormous amounts of pain.
Then I build section by section. Hero first. Services next. Then contact. Then I deal with responsiveness.
Is it slower than generating the whole site in one shot? Yes. But when something breaks, I know exactly where to look. That alone is worth it.

The prompt that actually helped me learn
Here’s the one I keep coming back to:
“Act like a senior developer teaching a beginner. Help me build this step by step. Before writing any code, explain the folder structure and what each file does. Write simple code first. Avoid extra libraries. After each step, tell me how to test whether it actually works.”
Notice it doesn’t just ask AI to code. It asks AI to teach.
That’s the difference.

One habit that changed everything
Before AI writes any component, I ask it to explain what it’s going to build.
“Before writing the React component, explain how it will work.”
If I don’t understand the explanation, I ask again. “Explain it like I’ve never used React.” Or just: “Simpler please.”
This feels almost too basic to mention. But if you can’t explain what code does, you can’t fix it when it breaks. That’s just the truth.

Prompting is not the real skill
Everyone talks about prompt engineering. It’s useful, sure.
But the deeper skill — the one that actually makes you a developer — is knowing what to check after AI gives you something.
Does it actually run? Is it responsive? Are there packages you don’t need? Can you explain what each part does? What breaks if you delete this component? Is this solution simple or is it doing too much?
AI generates options. You still need judgment.
AI writes code. You still need direction.
AI moves fast. You still need control.

Don’t skip the basics just because AI exists
Learn HTML — every website still has structure.
Learn CSS — layout, spacing, and responsiveness don’t happen by magic.
Learn JavaScript — interactivity needs logic.
Learn React — modern apps are built from components.
Learn Git — because you will break things, and you need a way back.
Learn deployment — a site on your laptop isn’t a real website.
AI can help with all of these. It can’t replace your understanding of them.

The honest version
AI-assisted development isn’t a shortcut around learning. It’s a faster road through it.
That difference is everything.
Use AI only to generate code, and you’ll ship projects quickly while staying weak as a developer.
Use AI to understand, debug, test, and improve — and you’ll learn faster than any previous generation of developers could.
So yes. AI can build your website fast.
But can you fix it when it breaks?
That’s where the real thing starts.
